title: Riven oak shingles
subtitle: Hand-riven wooden roof and wall shingles from green oak
excerpt: A shingle is a thin tapered piece of wood used as a roof or wall cladding tile. Oak riven with a froe and beetle produces shingles that are waterproof along the grain, flexible enough to follow roof curves, and will outlast sawn equivalents because the fibres run unbroken through each piece. The process is rapid once the technique is understood: one skilled froe worker can produce hundreds of shingles in a day.
sourceNotes: Oak shingle production documented in British and European rural building craft traditions; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885); surviving shingle-roofed structures in the UK and northern Europe.

glossaryTerms.definition: Split along the grain. A riven shingle has its fibres running along its full length, which makes it waterproof: water running down the shingle surface runs with the grain rather than across cut fibres that could wick moisture into the structure.
An L-shaped cleaving tool with a blade set perpendicular to a wooden handle. Struck into the end grain to initiate a split, then levered to guide the split along the grain of the billet.
Freshly felled wood still high in moisture content. Green oak rives more cleanly than seasoned; the wood splits with less resistance and with less tendency to run out of the grain.
A section of log prepared as the starting point for riving. For shingles, the billet is a short round section of oak trunk, quartered and rived into shingle blanks radially from the centre.

The gradual reduction in thickness from one end to the other. A shingle is thick at the butt (lower exposed edge) and thin at the head (upper hidden edge) so that it can lap over the shingle below and drain cleanly.

projectSchedule.title: Quarter and rive the billet
projectSchedule.body: Quarter the oak round with the axe and rive to rough shingle blanks with froe and beetle.

projectSchedule.title: Dress to taper on the shaving horse
projectSchedule.body: Drawknife each shingle blank to a consistent taper if desired, or use the riven blanks directly.

projectSchedule.title: Shingles seasoned: ready to fix
projectSchedule.body: A month of air-drying produces stable shingles. Fix to roof or wall using copper or stainless nails.

content.text: oak shingle is waterproof along its length in a way a sawn shingle is not: the fibres run from thick end to thin without interruption, and water running down the surface follows the grain rather than crossing cut fibres that might wick it sideways. The process is froe work repeated until the
oak rives cleanly; each shingle takes a few seconds once the rhythm is established.

A green oak log section (bolt), 30 to 40 cm long and at least 25 cm in diameter. The bolt must be straight-grained and free of large knots; knots prevent the froe following the grain and cause the split to run out. Quarter the bolt with the axe and splitting wedges before riving to shingles. Sweet chestnut at the same dimensions is an excellent alternative; it rives as cleanly as oak and is also naturally durable.

and a beetle mallet for riving. Splitting wedges and carving axe for quartering the bolt. A drawknife and shaving horse for dressing the shingles to taper (optional; the riven blanks can be used as-is if the split produces a natural taper). A leather strop for the froe.
A standard roof shingle is 30 to 40 cm long, 8 to 15 cm wide, and

from 9 to 12 mm thick at the butt (lower exposed edge) to 2 to 3 mm at the head (upper hidden edge). Wall shingles are typically shorter at 25 cm. The bolt length sets the shingle length; work out the target dimensions before splitting.

Stand the bolt on end on a solid splitting block. Drive splitting wedges into the end grain with the beetle to produce halves, then quarters. The quarters are the working billets for shingle riving. Each quarter should show a clean radial face; shingles are rived radially from the centre rather than tangentially.

to split each quarter into shingle-width blanks. Position the froe blade on the end grain at the intended shingle width from the outer face and drive it in with one beetle blow. Lever the froe handle to guide the split along the grain. Each shingle should peel from the quarter radially, following the medullary rays.

If the split runs toward the thin side (toward the edge of the quarter), lever the froe handle away from the thin side to steer the split back. If it runs toward the thick side, lever the other way. Reading and steering the split is the central skill of shingle riving; a good froe worker keeps all splits parallel and close to even thickness.

If the riven blank has a natural taper from the riving, it can be used as-is. If the faces are parallel (equal thickness throughout), dress one face to a taper on the shaving horse using the drawknife: clamp the blank with the butt end away from the body and take shaving passes from the butt end toward the head, gradually reducing thickness toward the head end. Check the taper angle by holding the shingle against a consistent reference (a marked card or a previously dressed shingle).

Stack the rived shingles in open air with spacer sticks (stickers) between each layer to allow air circulation. Season for at least one month before fixing. Green shingles fixed to a roof will shrink as they dry; if fixed without gaps they will buckle. Leave 3 to 5 mm of lateral gap between adjacent shingles to allow for shrinkage.

Fix shingles with copper or stainless-steel nails only; galvanised nails will react with the tannins in oak and stain the shingles. Two nails per shingle, positioned in the concealed upper zone that will be covered by the next course above. The standard exposure for a 35 cm shingle is 12 to 14 cm (one-third to two-thirds of the length, depending on roof pitch and rainfall).

Make a decorative edge profile on the butt of each shingle by cutting a simple arc or chamfer with the sloyd knife before installation. A decorative butt edge was common on traditional Scandinavian shingled buildings.

Use riven shingles as wall cladding (horizontal weatherboard style) rather than roofing: the same process, fixed horizontally with a larger overlap since wall exposure to rain is different from roof exposure.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when riving and dressing shingles.

items.symptom: The split runs out at an angle rather than following the grain
items.cause: The bolt has a spiral grain, or a knot is deflecting the split.
items.fix: Lever the froe handle in the direction toward which the split is running to try to steer it back. If the grain is too irregular, reject the bolt section for shingles and use it for firewood; spiral-grain wood cannot produce reliable shingles.

items.symptom: Shingles cup across the width after drying
items.cause: The shingle was rived tangentially rather than radially; tangential shrinkage is greater than radial, causing cupping.
items.fix: Check the riving direction: shingles should be rived radially, with the growth rings running roughly perpendicular to the shingle face. Re-split the quartered bolt to produce radial pieces.

items.symptom: The drawknife taper is uneven across the width of the shingle
items.cause: The shaving passes were not kept parallel across the width.
items.fix: Check the taper at both edges of each shingle with a marking gauge. Take corrective drawknife passes on the thick edge until the taper is consistent from edge to edge.
